content.full_text:
FPPC
AUG 5 1992
F

July 30,1992

CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL
PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J. STREET-SUITE 800                                RE: CANDIDATES
P.O. BOX 807                                              STATEMENTS
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA                              IN
95804-0807                                            ELECTION BALLOTS

MARK T. MICRODOMI, COUNSEL
ENFORCEMENT DIVISION

DEAR MR. MICRODOMI:

I WOULD APPRECIATE THE OPINION OF THE CFPPC
ON THE FOLLOWING ISSUE.

FACTS
1-  THE CITY OF BURBANKS HISTORY ON ELECTIONS --
    CANDIDATE STATEMENT HAS ALWAYS BEEN
    THAT THE PRINTING COSTS, CIRCULATION, POSTAGE, ETC.
    WAS PAID FOR BY THE CITY TO KEEP THE
    PUBLIC IN FORMED.

2-  IN 1989 PROPOSITION 73 WAS INTERPRETAIED
    BY OUR CITY ATTORNEY THAT BURBANK COULD
    NO LONGER PAY THIS COST FOR CANDIDATES STATEMENTS
    (SIMPLE BALLOTS)

3-  ON MARCH 14, 1989 THE CITY ATTORNEY
    BASED ON A CFPP RULING REVERSED THIS
    DECISION AND REFUNDS FOR ALL COSTS
    PAID FOR BY THE CANDIDATES WERE ISSUED.
    (SAMPLE BALLOTS)
    (SEE ATTACHED)

[The image appears to be a blank lined notebook page with very faint handwriting that is largely illegible due to the image quality and contrast. Only scattered marks and faint text are visible throughout the page, but the specific words cannot be reliably made out. The page has standard horizontal ruled lines and a vertical margin line on the left side.]

-2-

I BELIEVE THIS ACTION SET A LEGAL
PRECEDENT THAT ALL FUTURE ELECTION
CANDIDATES SHOULD HAVE THE SAME CONSIDERATION.

NOT TO FOLLOW THIS PROCEDURE WOULD MEAN
THAT OTHER CANDIDATES RECEIVED SPECIAL
TREATMENT-A GIFT OF PUBLIC FUNDS.

I WOULD APPRECIATE THE CALIFORNIA FAIR
POLITICAL PRACTICES LEGAL OPINION.

THANK YOU FOR YOUR TIME.

H. Dianne Adams
D. Dianne Adams

1711 N. GRISMER #30
BURBANK, CALIFORNIA
91504

[The image appears to be a blank lined notebook page with very faint, illegible handwriting. The text is too light and unclear to accurately transcribe any specific words or content.]

OFFICE OF CITY ATTORNEY

RECEIVED
1989 MAR 16 AM 8:12

DATE: March 14, 1989

CITY CLERK
CITY OF BURBANK

TO: Hon. Mayor and Members of the
Burbank City Council

SUBJECT: FPPC Regs/Ballot Statements
Our File: 204

Last Tuesday, March 7, 1989, the Fair Political Practices'
Commission rescinded a previous regulation prohibiting the use of
public funds for financing candidates' statements in sample
ballots. The new FPPC regulation would allow a governmental entity to fund candidates
expressly allow a governmental entity to fund candidates
statements. Thus, it is in the discretion of the City Council
to fund the publication of candidates' statements in the sample
ballots for any and all future elections.

The original prohibition on the use of governmental funds for
financing candidates' statements stemmed from the very strict
interpretation the FPPC was taking in regard to provisions of
Proposition 73 adopted in June of 1988. Proposition 73
established certain limits on campaign contributions for
candidates for state and local elective offices and prohibited
the use of public funds for these campaign expenditures.
Specifically, Government Code Section 85300, one of the several
provisions added by Proposition 73, provides "no public officer
shall expend and no candidate shall accept any public monies for
the purpose of seeking elective office."

All government entities had been following the FPPC's lead and
strictly construing the above language to absolutely prohibit any
governmental contributions or assistance toward any campaign
governmental activities or campaign. In light of the recent FPPC vacillation
on public financing of candidates' statements, however, this
strict construction approach can be called into question.

It is the opinion of this Office that Proposition 73 prohibits
the payment of public funds to any candidate or any political
committee, either used political committee, or political party.
It is our opinion that the intent of the statute is to prohibit
the use of public funds for purposes to be determined at the
direction of a specific candidate or a political committee. In
other words, the public funds can't be used at the discretion of
an individual candidate or political committee. Public funds,
however, could be used to fund non-discretionary programs.

City Council
-FPCC-
Page 2

available to all candidates on an equal and non-discriminatory
basis. Public funding of candidates' statements, for example,
would be an example of such a program. In addition, and more
importantly, it would also be our opinion that the City could
fund the televising of candidates' forums. Such candidate
forums, however, must be non-discriminatory, open and available
to all candidates under uniform and consistent rules and
regulations.

If you have any questions, please do not hesitate to give me a
call.

[signature]

DOUGLAS C. HOLLAND
City Attorney

DCH/er
DHRCOUNCIL33

cc: R. Ovrom, City Manager
    M. Richards, Library Services Director

THE BURBANK
LEADER

A-4 The Burbank Leader, Saturday, March 25, 1989

Election candidates to get
money back for statements

By LINDA F. LOZANO
Staff Writer

Candidates who vied for spots in
city government during the primary
election will get a refund of the
money they paid for their voter
statements.

The City Council approved the
refunds Tuesday and also decided
to pay for candidate statements in
the upcoming general election on
April 13.

The money will be refunded to
12 candidates who paid $313, with

one paying $500, to include their
statements on the ballot.

Council will also reimburse the
two sponsors of the televised debate on cable TV.

City Manager Robert Ovrom
said Prop. 73, which [illegible]
campaign spending, gives cities the
option to pay for those items.

sections.facts:
1- The City of Burbank's history on elections -- candidate statements has always been that the printing costs, circulation, postage, etc., was paid for by the city to keep the public informed.

2- In 1989 Proposition 73 was interpreted by our City Attorney that Burbank could no longer pay this cost for candidates statements (sample ballots).

3- On March 14, 1989 the City Attorney based on a C.F.P.P. ruling reversed this decision and refunds for all costs paid for by the candidates were issued. (Sample Ballots)

(See attached)

The image you provided is a blank sheet of lined paper with no visible handwritten or printed text. If you have another page or another section of the document, please provide it for better results.

I believe this action set a legal precedent that all future election candidates should have the same consideration.

Not to follow this procedure would mean that other candidates received special treatment—a gift of public funds.

I would appreciate the California Fair Political Practices Legal Opinion.

Thank you for your time.

D. Dianne Adams
1711 No. Grismer #30
Burbank, California 91504

OFFICE OF CITY ATTORNEY

DATE: March 14, 1989

TO: Hon. Mayor and Members of the Burbank City Council

SUBJECT: FPPC Regs/Ballot Statements
Our File: 204

RECEIVED
1989 MAR 16 AM 8:12
CITY CLERK
CITY OF BURBANK

Last Tuesday, March 7, 1989, the Fair Political Practices' Commission rescinded a previous regulation prohibiting the use of public funds for financing candidates' statements in sample ballots. The new FPPC regulation, adopted last week, would expressly allow a governmental entity to fund candidates' statements. Thus, it is in the discretion of the City Council to fund the publication of candidates' statements in the sample ballots for any and all future elections.

The original prohibition on the use of governmental funds for financing candidates' statements stemmed from the very strict interpretation the FPPC was taking in regard to provisions of Proposition 73 adopted in June of 1988. Proposition 73 established certain limits on campaign contributions for candidates for state and local elective offices and prohibited the use of public funds for these campaign expenditures. Specifically, Government Code Section 85300, one of the several provisions added by Proposition 73, provides "no public officer shall expend and no candidate shall accept any public monies for the purpose of seeking elective office."

All government entities had been following the FPPC's lead and strictly construing the above language to absolutely prohibit any governmental contributions or assistance toward election activities or coverage. In light of the recent FPPC vacillation on public financing of candidates' statements, however, this strict construction approach can be called into question.

It is the opinion of this Office that Proposition 73 prohibits the payment of public funds to any candidate or any political committee, broad based political committee, or political party. It is our opinion that the intent of the statute is to prohibit the use of public funds for purposes to be determined at the discretion of a specific candidate or a political committee. In other words, the public funds can't be used at the discretion of an individual candidate or political committee. Public funds, however, could be used to fund non-discretionary programs.

available to all candidates on an equal and non-discriminatory basis. Public funding of candidates' statements, for example, would be an example of such a program. In addition, and more importantly, it would also be our opinion that the City could fund the televising of candidates' forums. Such candidate forums, however, must be non-discriminatory, open and available to all candidates under uniform and consistent rules and regulations.

embedding.qa_text: July 30, 1992 FPPC AUG-5 1992 California Fair Political Practices Commission 428 J. Street - Suite 800 P.O. Box 807 Sacramento, California 95804-0807 RE: Candidates Statements in Election Ballots Mark T. Morodomi, Counsel Enforcement Division Dear Mr. Morodomi: I would appreciate the opinion of the C.F.P.P.C. on the following issue. FACTS 1- The City of Burbank's history on elections -- candidate statements has always been that the printing costs, circulation, postage, etc., was paid for by the city to keep the public informed. 2- In 1989 Proposition 73 was interpreted by our City Attorney that Burbank could no longer pay this cost for candidates statements (sample ballots). 3- On March 14, 1989 the City Attorney based on a C.F.P.P. ruling reversed this decision and refunds for all costs paid for by the candidates were issued. (Sample Ballots) (See attached) The image you provided is a blank sheet of lined paper with no visible handwritten or printed text. If you have another page or another section of the document, please provide it for better results. I believe this action set a legal precedent that all future election candidates should have the same consideration. Not to follow this procedure would mean that other candidates received special treatment—a gift of public funds. I would appreciate the California Fair Political Practices Legal Opinion. Thank you for your time. D. Dianne Adams 1711 No. Grismer #30 Burbank, California 91504 The image consists of handwritten text on lined paper, but the handwriting is extremely faint and mostly illegible. Only a few scattered letters and numbers are partially visible, such as "T", "A", "E", "O", "N", "S", "H", "I", "C", "L", "U", "R", "E", "D", "A", "T", "O", "O", "L", "A", "T", "O", "G", "S", "H", "I", "C", "L", "U", "R", "E", "D", "A", "T", "O", "O", "L", "A", "T", "O", "G", "S", "H", "I", "C", "L", "U", "R", "E", "D", "A", "T", "O", "O", "L", "A", "T", "O", "G", "S", "H", "I", "C", "L", "U", "R", "E", "D", "A", "T", "O", "O", "L", "A", "T", "O", "G", "S", "H", "I", "C", "L", "U", "R", "E", "D", "A", "T", "O", "O", "L", "A", "T", "O", "G", "S", "H", "I", "C", "L", "U", "R", "E", "D", "A", "T", "O", "O", "L", "A", "T", "O", "G", "S", "H", "I", "C", "L", "U", "R", "E", "D", "A", "T", "O", "O", "L", "A", "T", "O", "G", "S", "H", "I", "C", "L", "U", "R", "E", "D", "A", "T", "O", "O", "L", "A", "T", "O", "G", "S", "H", "I", "C", "L", "U", "R", "E", "D", "A", "T", "O", "O", "L", "A", "T", "O", "G", "S", "H", "I", "C", "L", "U", "R", "E", "D", "A", "T", "O", "O", "L", "A", "T", "O", "G", "S", "H", "I", "C", "L", "U", "R", "E", "D", "A", "T", "O", "O", "L", "A", "T", "O", "G", "S", "H", "I", "C", "L", "U", "R", "E", "D", "A", "T", "O", "O", "L", "A", "T", "O", "G", "S", "H", "I", "C", "L", "U", "R", "E", "D", "A", "T", "O", "O", "L", "A",
